label: Phase 2 Questions — Red Flags & Safety (np-p2-001 through np-p2-062)
difficultyPolicy: hard or expert only — no medium

questions.stemAnchor: BP 210/118 + headache + altered mental status + papilledema — hypertensive emergency vs urgency disposition
questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with known hypertension presents to your clinic reporting a severe headache and 'feeling confused' since this morning. His wife reports he has been 'not himself' for 3 hours. BP is 210/118 mmHg, HR 88, RR 18, SpO2 98%. Fundoscopic examination reveals bilateral blurred disc margins and flame hemorrhages. He is oriented to person only. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.A: Administer oral labetalol 200 mg and reassess blood pressure in 2 hours
options.B: Administer sublingual nifedipine to rapidly reduce blood pressure
options.C: Call 911 for emergent transfer to the ED for IV antihypertensive therapy
options.D: Schedule an urgent next-day follow-up with neurology and begin amlodipine

questions.rationale: This is a hypertensive emergency — BP ≥180/120 with acute end-organ damage (hypertensive encephalopathy: altered mental status + papilledema + flame hemorrhages). Hypertensive emergency requires IV antihypertensive therapy (nitroprusside, nicardipine, or labetalol IV) in a monitored setting — oral agents are inadequate. Option A (oral labetalol) is appropriate for urgency, not emergency. Option B (sublingual nifedipine) is contraindicated — it causes precipitous, uncontrolled BP drops and is no longer used for any hypertensive emergency. Option D delays appropriate care. The NP must call 911 immediately.

questions.stemAnchor: BP 188/110 + no symptoms + headache x2 days — urgency vs emergency triage decision
questions.stem: A 44-year-old woman with hypertension (on amlodipine 5 mg) calls your clinic after checking her BP at a pharmacy — BP is 188/110 mmHg on two readings. She reports a mild headache for the past two days, which she attributes to stress. She has no chest pain, shortness of breath, vision changes, or confusion. She walks to the clinic immediately. Repeat BP in clinic is 184/108 mmHg. Neurological exam is normal; fundoscopic exam shows sharp disc margins. Which of the following best describes her clinical status and most appropriate management?

options.A: Hypertensive emergency requiring 911 activation and IV antihypertensive therapy
options.B: Hypertensive urgency; oral medication adjustment with reassessment within 24 hours is appropriate
options.C: Stage 2 hypertension requiring immediate ED transfer regardless of symptoms
options.D: BP measurement artifact; have her rest 10 minutes and discharge without intervention

questions.rationale: Hypertensive urgency is defined as BP ≥180/120 mmHg WITHOUT evidence of acute end-organ damage. This patient has a BP of 184/108–188/110 with only mild headache, a normal neurological exam, and no papilledema. Urgency does NOT require ED transfer — outpatient oral medication adjustment (e.g., increasing amlodipine, adding a second agent) with reassessment within 24 hours is appropriate. Option A (emergency) requires evidence of acute organ damage (encephalopathy, papilledema, AKI, acute HF, aortic dissection, NSTEMI) — absent here. Option C overcalls the urgency. Option D fails to address a clinically significant BP level requiring intervention.

questions.stemAnchor: Young woman + resistant HTN + spontaneous hypokalemia K 2.9 + BP uncontrolled on 3 agents — secondary HTN workup
questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman presents for a medication review. She was diagnosed with hypertension at age 24 and is currently on lisinopril 40 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, and chlorthalidone 25 mg daily, yet her BP remains 158/96 mmHg. Basic metabolic panel shows: Na 142, K 2.9, CO2 30, Cr 0.8. She denies diarrhea, vomiting, or diuretic overuse. She does not take NSAIDs or oral contraceptives. Physical exam reveals no abdominal bruit and no cushingoid features. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to evaluate her resistant hypertension?

options.A: Increase chlorthalidone to 50 mg daily and add spironolactone 25 mg
options.B: Obtain aldosterone-to-renin ratio (ARR) to screen for primary aldosteronism
options.C: Order 24-hour urine cortisol to screen for Cushing syndrome
options.D: Obtain plasma free metanephrines to screen for pheochromocytoma

questions.rationale: This patient has resistant hypertension (uncontrolled on 3 agents including a diuretic) at a young age with spontaneous hypokalemia (K 2.9 without diuretic overuse or GI losses) — the classic pattern of primary aldosteronism (Conn syndrome). The initial screening test is the aldosterone-to-renin ratio (ARR); an ARR >20–30 with plasma aldosterone >15 ng/dL is suspicious. Primary aldosteronism is the most common cause of secondary hypertension. Option A adds an aldosterone antagonist without confirming the diagnosis. Option C (Cushing screen) is appropriate when proximal muscle weakness, central obesity, purple striae, and moon facies are present — absent here. Option D (pheochromocytoma screen) is indicated for paroxysmal hypertension with classic triad — absent here.

questions.stemAnchor: BP 178/112 + sudden onset tearing chest/back pain + unequal arm BP 24 mmHg — aortic dissection vs hypertensive emergency
questions.stem: A 67-year-old man with longstanding hypertension presents to your urgent care clinic with sudden-onset severe chest pain radiating to his back that began 40 minutes ago. He describes the pain as 'tearing' and says it was worst at onset. BP right arm 178/112 mmHg; BP left arm 154/88 mmHg. HR 104. He is diaphoretic and appears in pain. D-dimer is 1.8 µg/mL (elevated). ECG shows sinus tachycardia without ST changes. Which of the following is the most appropriate next action?

options.A: Initiate IV nicardipine to lower BP and contact nephrology for hypertensive emergency
options.B: Administer aspirin 324 mg and activate STEMI protocol
options.C: Call 911 immediately for emergent transfer; suspected aortic dissection requires CT aortography in the ED
options.D: Order urgent troponin and repeat ECG in 30 minutes to evaluate for NSTEMI

questions.rationale: The clinical picture is highly concerning for aortic dissection: sudden tearing chest-back pain maximal at onset, interarm BP difference of 24 mmHg (>20 mmHg = significant), diaphoresis, and elevated D-dimer. Aortic dissection is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate CT aortography for diagnosis. The diagnosis must be established before treatment decisions. Option A (nicardipine) is premature without confirmed diagnosis and should not delay transfer. Option B (aspirin + STEMI protocol) is dangerous — anticoagulation/thrombolytics in aortic dissection are potentially lethal. Option D delays life-saving diagnosis. Call 911 immediately; do not delay for additional in-office testing.

questions.stemAnchor: Pregnant 32 weeks + BP 164/108 + headache + visual changes — eclampsia pre-eclampsia distinction
questions.stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 at 32 weeks gestation is seen in your outpatient clinic for a routine prenatal visit. She reports a headache for the past 2 days and describes 'seeing flashing lights.' BP today is 164/108 mmHg (previously normotensive). Urine dipstick: 2+ proteinuria. She has no clonus but reports right upper quadrant discomfort. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.A: Start oral labetalol 200 mg and schedule return visit in one week
options.B: Initiate magnesium sulfate and call 911 for emergent OB transfer
options.C: Reassure her that mild headaches and floaters are common in pregnancy and no action is needed
options.D: Order ambulatory blood pressure monitoring and recheck in 24 hours

questions.rationale: This patient meets criteria for severe-range preeclampsia with severe features: BP ≥160/110, proteinuria, headache, visual disturbances, and RUQ pain. The presence of severe features (BP ≥160/110 + neurological or hepatic symptoms) constitutes an obstetric emergency with imminent eclampsia risk. Magnesium sulfate provides seizure prophylaxis and must be initiated; emergent OB transfer is required. Option A (oral labetalol alone) is insufficient for severe features and delays definitive management. Option C dismisses clearly severe features. Option D is inappropriate — this is not a monitoring situation; this is an emergency requiring immediate escalation.

questions.stemAnchor: Paroxysmal BP spikes 200+/120 + headache + palpitations + diaphoresis + episodic — pheochromocytoma recognition
questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with episodic severe headaches occurring 2–3 times weekly for the past 4 months. Each episode lasts 15–30 minutes and is associated with palpitations, profuse sweating, and a 'pounding feeling in her chest.' During a recent episode witnessed by her husband, she appeared flushed with a pulse of 130. She has no history of hypertension; her in-office BP today is 128/82. Basic labs are normal. Her primary care provider previously attributed the episodes to panic disorder. Which of the following screening test is most appropriate?

options.A: Ambulatory cardiac monitoring (Holter) to evaluate for paroxysmal arrhythmia
options.B: Plasma free metanephrines or 24-hour urine catecholamines/metanephrines
options.C: Aldosterone-to-renin ratio to screen for primary aldosteronism
options.D: 24-hour urine cortisol to evaluate for Cushing syndrome

questions.rationale: The classic pheochromocytoma triad is paroxysmal headache + palpitations + diaphoresis with episodic hypertension. The episodic nature, normal between-episode BP, and young patient should raise high suspicion. Plasma free metanephrines (most sensitive, >95%) or 24-hour urine fractionated metanephrines/catecholamines are the preferred screening tests. Aldosterone-to-renin ratio (C) is for sustained hypertension with hypokalemia. Cortisol (D) is for Cushing features. Holter monitoring (A) might detect arrhythmia but would miss the endocrine cause. This is a classic NP-level screening decision where anchoring on 'panic disorder' leads to a missed diagnosis.

questions.stemAnchor: Patient with multiple in-office high BP but normal home readings — white coat HTN ABPM indication
questions.stem: A 52-year-old man is referred for hypertension management. In-office BP has been consistently elevated at three visits: 146/92, 152/96, and 148/90 mmHg. He has been recording home readings twice daily for two weeks, which average 118/74 mmHg. He is currently on no antihypertensive medications. He is a non-smoker with no DM, no CKD, and no cardiovascular disease history. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Begin antihypertensive therapy with lisinopril 10 mg based on consistently elevated office readings
options.B: Order ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) to differentiate white coat hypertension from sustained hypertension
options.C: Reassure the patient that his normal home readings confirm normal blood pressure, and no further workup is needed
options.D: Diagnose masked hypertension and initiate combination antihypertensive therapy

questions.rationale: White coat hypertension (WCH) is defined as persistently elevated in-office BP with normal out-of-office BP readings. This patient's pattern (consistently elevated office BP, consistently normal home readings) is highly suggestive of WCH. ABPM is the gold standard to confirm the diagnosis. Starting antihypertensives without confirmation risks treating a normotensive patient and causing hypotension at home. Option C is incorrect because ABPM is still required — home readings alone, while suggestive, are not as validated as ABPM. Option D (masked hypertension) is the opposite pattern: normal in-office, elevated out-of-office — not applicable here. Patients with WCH still require monitoring as they have higher long-term risk of developing sustained HTN.

questions.stemAnchor: Older man on ACEi + new eGFR drop 45 to 28 after addition — bilateral renal artery stenosis recognition
questions.stem: A 71-year-old man with hypertension, T2DM, and CKD (baseline eGFR 45 mL/min) was started on lisinopril 10 mg 4 weeks ago. Repeat BMP today shows creatinine 2.1 mg/dL (was 1.4 mg/dL) with eGFR 28 mL/min. K+ is 5.4 mEq/L. He has no symptoms. BP is 140/88 mmHg. His abdominal aorta has a bruit audible on exam. Which of the following best explains his acute kidney injury and is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: ACEi-induced hyperkalemia is the primary problem; discontinue lisinopril and start amlodipine
options.B: This degree of creatinine rise is expected and acceptable with ACEi initiation in CKD; continue lisinopril
options.C: This presentation suggests bilateral renal artery stenosis; discontinue lisinopril and order renal artery Doppler ultrasound
options.D: Start IV normal saline for pre-renal azotemia and resume lisinopril after volume repletion

questions.rationale: An abdominal bruit + acute kidney injury after starting an ACEi with a creatinine rise >30% from baseline suggests bilateral renal artery stenosis (or stenosis of a single functioning kidney). In bilateral RAS, the ACEi removes efferent arteriole constriction that was maintaining GFR — the result is a precipitous GFR drop. A rise in creatinine ≤30% from baseline is acceptable and expected with ACEi in CKD; this patient's creatinine rose from 1.4 to 2.1 (50% increase), exceeding the threshold. Lisinopril must be discontinued and renal artery Doppler ultrasound ordered. If bilateral RAS is confirmed, ACEi/ARB are CONTRAINDICATED. Option B incorrectly accepts this degree of rise as normal.

questions.stemAnchor: 62-year-old with BP 190/115 + new severe headache but clear fundoscopy + normal neuro exam — urgency not emergency
questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with known hypertension (on metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily) presents to your clinic reporting her 'worst headache in years.' BP is 190/115 mmHg. She describes the headache as bilateral pressure, rated 7/10, without associated nausea, vomiting, visual changes, or confusion. Her neurological exam is entirely normal. Fundoscopic examination reveals sharp, distinct disc margins without hemorrhages or exudates. Which of the following represents the most appropriate management?

options.A: Call 911 for emergent transfer due to the patient's 'worst headache in years' (thunderclap concern)
options.B: Perform immediate LP to rule out subarachnoid hemorrhage before addressing blood pressure
options.C: Administer oral clonidine 0.2 mg, repeat BP in 1–2 hours, and arrange close follow-up within 24 hours
options.D: Start intravenous labetalol and begin BP lowering to <160/100 within 30 minutes

questions.rationale: This is hypertensive urgency: BP ≥180/120 without end-organ damage. Key: the 'worst headache in years' does NOT qualify as a thunderclap headache (which is maximal at onset in seconds — SAH pattern covered in Phase 1). This headache is bilateral pressure-type and the patient has a clear exam, normal fundoscopy, and no neurological deficits — consistent with hypertension-related headache, not SAH. For urgency, oral medication adjustment (e.g., oral clonidine, increasing existing antihypertensive, or adding a new oral agent) with reassessment within 24 hours is appropriate. Option A overcalls without true thunderclap features. Option B is not indicated without thunderclap/SAH concern. Option D (IV labetalol) is for hypertensive emergency only.

questions.stemAnchor: Obese patient + resistant HTN on 4 meds + snoring + daytime sleepiness — OSA as secondary HTN cause
questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with BMI 41 presents for follow-up of hypertension that has been difficult to control. He is currently on lisinopril 40 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, chlorthalidone 25 mg, and spironolactone 50 mg. His BP averages 148/92 mmHg. His wife reports he snores loudly and has witnessed apneas. He reports excessive daytime sleepiness (Epworth score 16/24). Aldosterone-to-renin ratio was negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to address his resistant hypertension?

options.A: Increase spironolactone to 100 mg and add hydralazine as the fifth antihypertensive
options.B: Refer for sleep study and initiation of CPAP therapy, which has been shown to reduce BP in OSA
options.C: Order 24-hour urine catecholamines to screen for pheochromocytoma
options.D: Refer to renal for consideration of denervation procedure

questions.rationale: Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) is one of the most common causes of resistant hypertension. This patient has a BMI of 41, loud snoring, witnessed apneas, and excessive daytime sleepiness (Epworth ≥10) — a clinically evident OSA presentation. Untreated OSA activates the sympathetic nervous system and RAAS, causing nocturnal hypertension and blunting antihypertensive drug efficacy. CPAP therapy is associated with modest but meaningful BP reduction in OSA with resistant HTN. The secondary HTN workup (ARR negative) has already been initiated. Option A escalates medications without addressing the underlying reversible cause. Option C (pheochromocytoma) lacks the episodic BP spikes and classic triad. Option D (renal denervation) is investigational and not recommended without exhausting reversible causes including OSA.

questions.stemAnchor: 78-year-old + sudden left facial droop + arm weakness + slurred speech lasting 12 minutes — TIA high-risk ABCD2 score, ED disposition
questions.stem: A 78-year-old man with hypertension, diabetes, and atrial fibrillation (on warfarin, INR 1.6 today) presents 35 minutes after a 12-minute episode of left facial drooping, left arm weakness, and slurred speech. All symptoms fully resolved before arrival. His INR today is 1.6 (subtherapeutic). BP is 168/94 mmHg. Neurological exam is now normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Schedule non-urgent MRI brain within 1 week and increase warfarin dose
options.B: Activate 911 for emergent transfer to stroke center; TIA with ABCD2 ≥6 requires same-day evaluation
options.C: Start aspirin 325 mg and schedule carotid duplex and echocardiogram within 48 hours
options.D: Reassure the patient that complete resolution of symptoms confirms this was a TIA and not a stroke; discharge with neurology follow-up in 1 month

questions.rationale: This patient has an ABCD2 score of 7: Age ≥60 (1), BP ≥140/90 (1), Clinical features = unilateral weakness (2), Duration 10–59 min (1), Diabetes (1) = 6 points, exceeding the high-risk threshold of ≥4. Additionally, he has a subtherapeutic INR on warfarin for AF (the likely cardioembolic source) — this represents a very high 48-hour stroke risk (>8%). The current standard of care requires same-day evaluation at a stroke center for TIA with ABCD2 ≥4 or any dual antiplatelet or anticoagulation consideration. Option A (1-week MRI) is dangerously delayed. Option C (aspirin) alone is inadequate — DAPT may be appropriate but his AF makes anticoagulation optimization the priority. Option D is wrong: complete resolution is expected with TIA; this does not reduce urgency.

questions.stemAnchor: 34-year-old + sudden severe vertigo + dysarthria + ipsilateral facial numbness + contralateral limb ataxia — posterior stroke vs labyrinthitis
questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset severe dizziness that began 2 hours ago and has not improved. She describes it as 'the room spinning.' She has difficulty walking without holding onto the wall. Neurological exam reveals: dysarthria, right-sided facial numbness, right Horner syndrome (ptosis, miosis), and left arm and leg ataxia. She has no nausea but appears anxious. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis and action?

options.A: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV); perform Epley maneuver
options.B: Acute labyrinthitis; start meclizine and follow up in 2 days
options.C: Posterior circulation ischemic stroke (likely PICA territory — Wallenberg syndrome); call 911 immediately
options.D: Vestibular migraine; administer IV prochlorperazine and observe 1 hour

questions.rationale: This is Wallenberg syndrome (lateral medullary syndrome from PICA occlusion): ipsilateral facial numbness, ipsilateral Horner syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis), contralateral limb sensory loss, dysarthria, dysphagia, and ipsilateral limb ataxia. This is a posterior circulation stroke, not vestibular disease. BPPV and labyrinthitis do not cause dysarthria, Horner syndrome, or sensory dissociation. The key distinguishing feature is the combination of bulbar signs with crossed sensory findings — this is a brainstem stroke. Call 911 immediately for CT followed by MRI and stroke team evaluation. Missing this diagnosis as 'labyrinthitis' or 'vestibular migraine' is a high-yield NP examination trap.

questions.stemAnchor: 45-year-old + low back pain + urinary retention + perianal numbness + bilateral leg weakness — cauda equina syndrome emergent MRI
questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with a 48-hour history of worsening low back pain. Today he reports difficulty starting urination and describes numbness in the area he 'sits on.' His wife notes he has been dragging both feet when walking. Examination reveals decreased anal sphincter tone, saddle anesthesia (bilateral perineal/perianal numbness), 4/5 strength in both lower extremities, and absent ankle jerks bilaterally. He had an MRI-confirmed L4-L5 disc herniation 6 months ago treated conservatively. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Prescribe cyclobenzaprine and methocarbamol with a follow-up in 1 week
options.B: Order an outpatient lumbar MRI within 72 hours and refer to orthopedics
options.C: Call 911 for immediate transfer; cauda equina syndrome requires emergent MRI and neurosurgical evaluation within hours
options.D: Start dexamethasone 10 mg IV and schedule an urgent neurology appointment for tomorrow

questions.rationale: Cauda equina syndrome (CES) is a surgical emergency. The classic presentation is saddle anesthesia, bladder/bowel dysfunction (urinary retention before incontinence), bilateral lower extremity weakness, and areflexia — all present here. Delay in diagnosis results in irreversible sphincter paralysis and permanent lower extremity neurological deficits. Emergent MRI (within hours) and neurosurgical evaluation are required. Option B (72-hour MRI) is far too delayed — CES hours matter. Option A (muscle relaxants) is dangerously inappropriate. Option D (dexamethasone) may have a role in spinal cord compression from malignancy but is not the primary action — transfer comes first.

questions.stemAnchor: Older woman + known breast cancer + 3 weeks progressive bilateral leg weakness + urine incontinence + mid-thoracic pain — malignant spinal cord compression
questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman with known breast cancer (on hormone therapy, last oncology visit 3 months ago) presents with a 3-week history of progressively worsening mid-thoracic back pain, bilateral leg weakness (now using a walker), and urinary incontinence that began yesterday. She has no fever. Neurological exam reveals 3/5 bilateral lower extremity strength, decreased sensation below the umbilicus, and bilateral hyperreflexia with positive Babinski signs. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.A: Start celecoxib 200 mg twice daily and refer to pain management within 1 week
options.B: Administer dexamethasone 10 mg IV immediately and call 911 for emergent transfer for MRI spine and radiation oncology evaluation
options.C: Schedule outpatient MRI thoracic spine within 48 hours and contact her oncologist
options.D: Refer to physical therapy for lower extremity strengthening and fall prevention

questions.rationale: This is malignant spinal cord compression (MSCC) — an oncological emergency. Features: known breast cancer (common source of bone/spine metastases) + progressive bilateral lower extremity weakness + sensory level + Babinski signs + new urinary incontinence + thoracic pain. Immediate dexamethasone (10–16 mg IV) reduces spinal cord edema and preserves neurological function. Emergent MRI is required to confirm the level and extent. Radiation oncology (and possibly neurosurgery) must be contacted for emergent treatment. Option C (48-hour MRI) would allow permanent paralysis to develop. Option D is inappropriate for a neurological emergency. Time to treatment is the key determinant of neurological outcome — each hour of delay matters.

questions.stemAnchor: 52-year-old diabetic + sudden left hand weakness + face normal + speech normal + resolves — lacunar TIA motor strip territory
questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with hypertension and poorly controlled T2DM presents 90 minutes after a 20-minute episode of sudden, isolated left hand and forearm weakness. There was no facial involvement, no language disturbance, and no vision change. The episode resolved completely. He had a similar but shorter episode 3 weeks ago that he 'forgot to mention.' BP is 178/102 mmHg. Neurological exam is now entirely normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate response?

options.A: Diagnose as peripheral nerve entrapment (carpal tunnel syndrome) and refer to neurology as outpatient within 3 weeks
options.B: Initiate dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin + clopidogrel and discharge with urgent neurology follow-up in 48 hours
options.C: Activate 911 for emergent stroke center evaluation; recurrent TIA with high ABCD2 score requires same-day evaluation
options.D: Order outpatient carotid duplex and brain MRI within 1 week; start aspirin and increase antihypertensives

questions.rationale: This patient has had TWO episodes of focal neurological deficit — the prior event 3 weeks ago and the current event. Recurrent TIA dramatically increases 48-hour stroke risk. ABCD2 score: Age 52 (0), BP ≥140/90 (1), Clinical = unilateral weakness (2), Duration 10–59 min (1), DM (1) = 5 points — high risk. Recurrence raises this further. Same-day stroke center evaluation is required. Dual antiplatelet (POINT/CHANCE trials) may be appropriate but must be initiated in the context of a stroke center evaluation with imaging to exclude hemorrhage. Option A (carpal tunnel) is incorrect — isolated limb motor TIA from a contralateral cortical or lacunar source does not cause carpal tunnel pattern. Option D delays evaluation inappropriately for a recurrent TIA patient.

questions.stemAnchor: Ascending bilateral flaccid weakness + areflexia + 10 days post-Campylobacter diarrhea — Guillain-Barré respiratory threat
questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with bilateral leg weakness that began 5 days ago and has progressed to involve her arms. She had a bout of bloody diarrhea 10 days prior that resolved without treatment. Examination reveals 2/5 lower extremity strength, 3/5 upper extremity strength, bilateral areflexia, and diminished pin sensation in her feet. She has no fever. Vital signs: SpO2 96%, RR 22. She reports shortness of breath when lying flat. Which of the following is the most critical next action?

options.A: Order electromyography (EMG) and nerve conduction studies as an urgent outpatient test within 48 hours
options.B: Start empiric intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) in the clinic and schedule follow-up in 3 days
options.C: Call 911 immediately; this clinical picture is consistent with Guillain-Barré syndrome with impending respiratory failure
options.D: Start oral prednisone 60 mg/day and monitor in clinic for 2 hours before discharge decision

questions.rationale: Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS): ascending bilateral flaccid weakness + areflexia + preceding Campylobacter infection (most common GBS trigger). SpO2 of 96% with RR 22 and orthopnea suggests early respiratory compromise. GBS can progress to respiratory failure within hours — the critical clinical marker is respiratory muscle involvement. Bedside assessment: inability to count to 20 in one breath, NIF <−25 cmH₂O, or FVC <20 mL/kg = ICU admission criteria. The NP must call 911 immediately. Option B (IVIG in clinic) is premature and unsafe without ICU monitoring capability. Option A (outpatient EMG in 48 hours) would allow life-threatening respiratory failure. Oral prednisone (D) is NOT indicated in GBS — it is ineffective and may worsen outcomes.

questions.stemAnchor: 22-year-old + first generalized convulsion + post-ictal confusion + no prior history — first seizure workup disposition
questions.stem: A 22-year-old college student is brought in by friends after a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately 3 minutes. He had 1–2 minutes of tonic-clonic activity and then was confused and sleepy for 20 minutes (post-ictal). He has no prior seizure history, takes no medications, and reports he was studying all night and skipped meals. He is now awake, oriented, and has a normal neurological exam. There was no alcohol or drug use. Glucose is 98 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Prescribe levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily empirically and schedule routine neurology follow-up
options.B: Discharge home with instructions to avoid driving and follow up with primary care within 1 week
options.C: Refer for urgent brain MRI and EEG within 24–48 hours; advise no driving until clearance; do NOT start AED empirically
options.D: Administer lorazepam 2 mg IV and observe for 4 hours, then discharge if no recurrence

questions.rationale: A first unprovoked seizure in a young adult with normal post-ictal exam and normal glucose requires urgent neurological workup (MRI brain, EEG) to identify a structural or epileptiform cause before committing to AED therapy. The NP's role is: initiate workup + refer neurology + advise driving restriction (legal in most states until seizure-free for 6 months). AEDs are NOT started empirically after a single unprovoked seizure without EEG/imaging — starting one without neurology evaluation is outside NP scope without specialist guidance. Option A (start levetiracetam) is inappropriate without confirming epilepsy diagnosis. Option B (wait 1 week) is too delayed — urgent (24–48h) workup is required. Option D (lorazepam) is for active/prolonged seizure — not needed once seizure has self-terminated.

questions.stemAnchor: 19-year-old + fever + stiff neck + petechial rash — meningococcemia LP timing vs antibiotics first
questions.stem: A 19-year-old college student is brought in by his roommate. He has had severe headache, fever to 39.8°C, and stiff neck for 8 hours. On exam you note a petechial-to-purpuric rash on his lower extremities and trunk. He is lethargic but arousable. BP 102/68 mmHg. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate action sequence?

options.A: Obtain non-contrast CT head to rule out mass before performing LP, then give antibiotics
options.B: Perform LP immediately to confirm meningococcal meningitis, then begin ceftriaxone
options.C: Administer ceftriaxone 2g IV immediately and call 911 for emergency transfer — do NOT delay antibiotics for imaging or LP
options.D: Start oral ciprofloxacin while calling for transport to avoid needle procedures in clinic

questions.rationale: Meningococcal meningitis (Neisseria meningitidis) is a medical emergency with a mortality rate that increases significantly with each hour of delay in antibiotics. The purpuric rash is characteristic — a clinical diagnosis trigger for immediate empiric ceftriaxone 2g IV (or IM if IV access not available). The principle is: antibiotics first, then diagnostic workup (LP, CT). A 30-minute delay for CT or LP while withholding antibiotics is associated with measurable mortality increase. In-clinic LP is not appropriate for a hemodynamically compromised meningococcal patient. Option A delays antibiotics for CT. Option B delays antibiotics for LP. Option D (oral ciprofloxacin) has no role — IV antibiotics are required for meningococcal disease.

questions.stemAnchor: 63-year-old with confusion + ophthalmoplegia + ataxia + alcohol history — Wernicke encephalopathy thiamine first
questions.stem: A 63-year-old man with known alcohol use disorder is brought by EMS after being found confused at home. He has consumed approximately 12 drinks per day for 20 years. Examination reveals: confusion (disoriented to place/time), bilateral lateral gaze palsy, and unsteady gait with wide-based stance. BP is 110/70, HR 104, temperature 37.1°C, glucose 88 mg/dL. Which of the following is the MOST critical immediate intervention?

options.A: Administer dextrose 50% IV immediately to treat possible hypoglycemia
options.B: Administer thiamine 500 mg IV before any glucose administration
options.C: Order CT head with contrast and start broad-spectrum antibiotics for possible meningoencephalitis
options.D: Give naloxone 0.4 mg IV to reverse possible opioid coingestant causing altered mental status

questions.rationale: Wernicke encephalopathy (WE) presents with the classic triad: confusion + ophthalmoplegia (lateral gaze palsy is the most common finding — nystagmus, or CN VI palsy) + ataxia. In any malnourished or alcohol use disorder patient with altered mental status, thiamine must be administered BEFORE glucose. Glucose administration without thiamine in a thiamine-deficient patient can precipitate or worsen Wernicke encephalopathy by depleting the remaining thiamine stores needed for glucose metabolism. Standard protocol: thiamine 500 mg IV first, then glucose. Option A is dangerous without thiamine first. Options C and D address other diagnoses not supported by the classic triad — but even in an uncertain presentation in an alcoholic patient, thiamine should be given first.

questions.stemAnchor: 30-year-old + new psychosis + fever + agitation + positive Babinski + CSF PCR — HSV encephalitis acyclovir before confirmation
questions.stem: A 30-year-old previously healthy woman is brought in by her husband after 4 days of progressive behavioral change, new-onset visual hallucinations, and a new generalized seizure this morning. She had a mild upper respiratory illness 2 weeks prior. Temp 38.4°C, BP 138/88, HR 106. Neurological exam: agitated, confused, with focal slowing on temporal EEG pattern reported from the ED. MRI is pending. CSF from LP is slightly turbid; RBCs 15/mm³, WBCs 120 (lymphocytic predominance), protein 82 mg/dL, glucose 52 mg/dL (serum 110 mg/dL). Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological action pending CSF PCR results?

options.A: Administer IV ceftriaxone + vancomycin for bacterial meningitis coverage
options.B: Start IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours empirically for HSV encephalitis
options.C: Begin high-dose IV methylprednisolone for autoimmune encephalitis pending anti-NMDAR antibody results
options.D: Withhold all treatment pending PCR results since antibiotics are ineffective against viral encephalitis

questions.rationale: This is HSV encephalitis until proven otherwise: fever + behavioral change + temporal lobe involvement (temporal EEG slowing) + lymphocytic CSF pleocytosis with slightly elevated protein + recent viral illness (herpes can reactivate). HSV encephalitis has 70% mortality if untreated and substantial morbidity even when treated — acyclovir must be started empirically without waiting for CSF PCR (which takes 24–72 hours). Starting acyclovir does not preclude adding bacterial coverage (option A can be added if bacterial meningitis is also on the differential), but HSV encephalitis is the primary concern in this young patient with temporal involvement. Option D (withhold all treatment) is dangerously wrong. Option C (methylprednisolone) may be considered after ruling out viral and bacterial causes — not as first action.

questions.stemAnchor: 40-year-old + new-onset psychosis workup — medical causes of psychosis CBC CMP TSH B12 RPR HIV urine drug screen
questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with no prior psychiatric history is brought by his wife. Over the past 3 weeks he has become increasingly paranoid, believes neighbors are 'spying' on him, and had an episode of hearing voices that told him to leave the house. He has had no fever, no headache, no seizures. He is alert, oriented to person and place (but not time), and not confused between questions. His wife denies any illicit drug use but reports he has been 'acting weird' even when sober. Which of the following initial workup is most appropriate before attributing this presentation to a primary psychiatric disorder?

options.A: Initiate risperidone 2 mg daily and refer to psychiatry for outpatient evaluation next week
options.B: Perform CBC, CMP, TSH, B12, RPR, HIV, urine drug screen, and brain MRI before initiating antipsychotic therapy
options.C: Diagnose bipolar disorder with psychotic features and start a mood stabilizer empirically
options.D: Refer to AA and substance use counseling as alcohol use disorder with alcoholic hallucinosis is most likely

questions.rationale: New-onset psychosis in middle age requires a medical workup to exclude organic causes before diagnosing a primary psychiatric disorder. The differential for organic psychosis includes: CNS infections (HIV, syphilis, HSV), autoimmune encephalitis, B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, hepatic encephalopathy, illicit drug use, and structural brain lesion. The workup includes CBC, CMP, TSH, B12, RPR, HIV, urine drug screen, and brain MRI. Initiating antipsychotics before ruling out a treatable medical cause (e.g., CNS syphilis, B12 deficiency) is inappropriate. Options A and C start psychiatric medications without workup. Option D (alcoholic hallucinosis) is possible but not confirmed — workup is required first.

questions.stemAnchor: Diabetic patient on metformin + CT contrast scheduled + eGFR 38 — contrast nephropathy prevention protocol
questions.stem: A 64-year-old woman with T2DM and CKD (eGFR 38 mL/min) is scheduled for a CT chest with contrast to evaluate a pulmonary nodule. She takes metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, and atorvastatin 40 mg. Which of the following represents the most appropriate pre-procedure medication management?

options.A: Continue all medications as usual; eGFR 38 is above the threshold for contrast concern
options.B: Hold metformin starting the day of contrast administration and do not restart for 48 hours post-procedure; ensure adequate hydration
options.C: Discontinue lisinopril 1 week prior to the procedure and give IV N-acetylcysteine the morning of the scan
options.D: Cancel the CT with contrast and order MRI without contrast as a safer alternative in all CKD patients

questions.rationale: The primary concern in this CKD patient receiving IV contrast is contrast-induced nephropathy (CIN). The management protocol: (1) Hold metformin on the day of contrast and for 48 hours post-procedure — metformin can accumulate if AKI occurs post-contrast, leading to lactic acidosis (rare but potentially fatal); (2) Ensure IV hydration (saline 1 mL/kg/hr before and after procedure); (3) Use the lowest contrast volume possible. Lisinopril (option C) holding is not the primary intervention. N-acetylcysteine evidence is mixed and not the primary action. Option D is inappropriate — contrast CT is appropriate if the indication is strong; CIN risk can be mitigated. Option A underestimates the risk — eGFR 38 (G3b) is in the range where metformin must be held.

questions.stemAnchor: 68-year-old male + iron-deficiency anemia + no obvious bleeding source + norcocytic — colorectal cancer workup
questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents for an annual well visit. CBC reveals: Hgb 10.2 g/dL, MCV 74 fL, WBC 7.2, platelets 312. He denies visible rectal bleeding, hematuria, or hemoptysis. He takes aspirin 81 mg daily and ibuprofen occasionally. He completed a FOBT (fecal occult blood test) that is positive on two of three samples. He has no family history of colorectal cancer. He underwent colonoscopy 10 years ago with 2 tubular adenomas removed. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Recheck CBC in 3 months after starting oral iron supplementation
options.B: Start oral iron supplementation and repeat FOBT in 1 year as routine screening
options.C: Order colonoscopy urgently — iron-deficiency anemia in an older man with positive FOBT and prior adenomas requires colorectal cancer exclusion
options.D: Attribute the anemia to NSAID use (ibuprofen); discontinue ibuprofen and recheck Hgb in 4 weeks

questions.rationale: Iron-deficiency anemia (microcytic, hypochromic) in an older male is a gastrointestinal cancer red flag until proven otherwise. This patient has: age 68, male sex, IDA, positive FOBT, and prior adenoma history — all independently elevating colorectal cancer (CRC) risk. NSAIDs can cause GI bleeding but do not account for the full clinical picture of IDA + positive FOBT + prior adenomas in a 68-year-old. The appropriate action is urgent colonoscopy, not deferring to iron supplementation or routine repeat FOBT. IDA in post-menopausal women or any male requires an upper and lower GI source to be excluded before attributing to dietary deficiency or NSAID use.

questions.stemAnchor: 55-year-old smoker + 15-lb weight loss + hemoptysis + CXR hilar mass — lung cancer workup
questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a 35-pack-year smoking history presents with an 8-week history of productive cough with streaks of blood, unintentional 15-pound weight loss over 3 months, and progressive fatigue. CXR shows a right hilar mass with ipsilateral mediastinal widening. He has no fever, and CBC is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Treat for community-acquired pneumonia with azithromycin and repeat CXR in 6 weeks
options.B: Order CT chest with contrast and refer urgently to pulmonology for bronchoscopic evaluation
options.C: Start empiric treatment for TB with RIPE therapy pending sputum cultures
options.D: Order PET scan as the first imaging step to avoid unnecessary radiation

questions.rationale: A heavy smoker with hemoptysis, significant weight loss, and a hilar mass on CXR has lung cancer until proven otherwise. The first step is CT chest with contrast (more sensitive and specific than CXR for mass characterization) followed by bronchoscopy for tissue diagnosis. Urgent pulmonology referral is appropriate. Treating with antibiotics (option A) is a common error that delays lung cancer diagnosis — a visible hilar mass should not be treated empirically as pneumonia. TB (option C) is possible (hemoptysis + weight loss) but requires sputum AFB before empiric RIPE; the hilar mass with mediastinal widening is more consistent with malignancy in a Western smoker. PET scan (option D) is typically ordered AFTER tissue diagnosis for staging, not as the first step.

questions.stemAnchor: 62-year-old + painless progressive dysphagia solids then liquids + 20 lb weight loss — esophageal cancer workup
questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with longstanding GERD (on PPI for 10 years) presents with progressive difficulty swallowing that began 3 months ago with solid foods and has now progressed to include soft foods. He has lost 20 pounds over 4 months without trying. He denies odynophagia but reports regurgitation of undigested food. He smokes one pack per day and drinks 2–3 glasses of wine daily. Which of the following is the most appropriate next action?

options.A: Double the PPI dose to twice daily and schedule follow-up in 6 weeks to assess dysphagia response
options.B: Order barium swallow as the first-line evaluation for dysphagia
options.C: Refer urgently for upper endoscopy (EGD) — progressive dysphagia with weight loss in a high-risk patient requires urgent cancer exclusion
options.D: Treat empirically for esophageal candidiasis with fluconazole given his PPI use history

questions.rationale: Progressive dysphagia (solids → liquids) + significant weight loss + risk factors (GERD for 10+ years with possible Barrett's, smoking, alcohol use) in a 62-year-old constitutes cancer alarm features. Upper endoscopy (EGD) is the definitive diagnostic test — it allows direct visualization and biopsy of the esophageal mucosa. Barium swallow (option B) can suggest but cannot biopsy a lesion — appropriate for dysphagia without alarm features but not the first choice with alarm features present. Doubling PPI (option A) is appropriate for uncomplicated GERD — not for alarm dysphagia. Fluconazole (option D) is for candidal esophagitis — typically occurs in immunocompromised patients with odynophagia, not obstructive progressive dysphagia.

questions.stemAnchor: 45-year-old woman + painless jaundice + weight loss + new-onset DM + CT shows pancreatic head mass — pancreatic cancer referral
questions.stem: A 45-year-old nonsmoking woman presents for evaluation of yellow discoloration of her skin and sclera that developed over 3 weeks. She denies abdominal pain. She has unintentionally lost 18 pounds over 3 months. Her primary care provider recently noted new-onset diabetes mellitus (A1c 7.8%) with no family history. Exam reveals scleral icterus, painless jaundice, and a non-tender, palpable gallbladder. Total bilirubin is 8.2 mg/dL (direct 6.9 mg/dL). LFTs show elevated alkaline phosphatase and GGT with normal transaminases. Which of the following best explains this presentation and the most appropriate next action?

options.A: Biliary colic; order abdominal ultrasound and hepatobiliary surgery referral for cholecystectomy
options.B: Autoimmune hepatitis; order ANA, anti-smooth muscle antibody, and liver biopsy
options.C: Pancreatic head malignancy (Courvoisier sign); order CT abdomen with pancreatic protocol and urgent gastroenterology/surgery referral
options.D: Acute viral hepatitis; order hepatitis A, B, C serologies and advise supportive care

questions.rationale: This presentation is classic for pancreatic head cancer: painless jaundice (obstructive: direct hyperbilirubinemia + elevated ALP/GGT), palpable non-tender gallbladder (Courvoisier sign — obstructed bile duct with a compliant, non-scarred gallbladder), significant weight loss, and new-onset DM (pancreatic exocrine/endocrine tissue destruction). CT abdomen with pancreatic protocol is the appropriate first imaging study. Urgent GI/surgery referral is needed. Biliary colic (option A) is painful and episodic, not painless progressive jaundice. Autoimmune hepatitis (option B) typically shows elevated transaminases. Viral hepatitis (option D) shows transaminase elevation, not the cholestatic pattern seen here.

questions.stemAnchor: 54-year-old + facial swelling + arm edema + distended neck veins + lung mass — SVC syndrome oncological emergency
questions.stem: A 54-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history and newly identified right upper lobe lung mass (on CT ordered for hemoptysis) presents to your clinic. He reports his face has become increasingly swollen over the past 2 weeks and his shirt collar feels tight. His arms are also swollen bilaterally. Exam reveals facial plethora, bilateral arm edema, distended non-pulsatile neck veins, and visible collateral veins across his chest. BP is 148/88 mmHg; SpO2 92% on room air. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Start furosemide 40 mg orally for bilateral arm and facial edema
options.B: Elevate his head of bed, restrict sodium, and return for follow-up in 1 week
options.C: Call 911 for emergent transfer — this presentation is consistent with superior vena cava syndrome, an oncological emergency
options.D: Order echocardiogram to evaluate for right heart failure as the cause of edema

questions.rationale: Superior vena cava (SVC) syndrome: facial/arm edema + distended neck veins + collateral chest wall veins + SpO2 92% in a patient with a known lung mass. This is an oncological emergency caused by mediastinal compression of the SVC (most commonly by lung cancer or lymphoma). Emergent oncology/radiation oncology consultation is required — radiation or stent placement can relieve SVC obstruction rapidly. Diuretics (option A) are ineffective and dangerous in SVC syndrome. Option B delays emergent care. Option D (echo) is not the priority — the diagnosis is clinical and the urgency requires immediate transfer, not outpatient cardiac imaging.

questions.stemAnchor: 32-year-old + fever + night sweats + 12% weight loss + non-tender rubbery supraclavicular node — lymphoma B-symptom workup
questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of progressive fatigue and night sweats that soak her bed linens. She has lost 14 pounds over 6 months without dieting. She noticed a lump above her right clavicle 3 weeks ago. Exam reveals a 2.5 cm, firm, non-tender, rubbery lymph node in the right supraclavicular fossa. She has no splenomegaly. LDH is 390 U/L (elevated). Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.A: Treat with azithromycin for 10 days for presumed atypical infection and recheck the node in 3 weeks
options.B: Perform fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of the supraclavicular node as the initial diagnostic procedure
options.C: Obtain CBC, LDH, uric acid, CXR, and refer urgently for excisional lymph node biopsy — this presentation meets criteria for lymphoma workup
options.D: Order chest CT and schedule follow-up in 4 weeks to ensure the node has not grown before deciding on biopsy

questions.rationale: This patient has lymphoma B symptoms (fever, drenching night sweats, ≥10% weight loss in 6 months) + right supraclavicular lymphadenopathy (always concerning — right supraclavicular: lung/mediastinal malignancy) + elevated LDH (marker of cell turnover). The combination mandates urgent workup and excisional lymph node biopsy. CBC, LDH, uric acid, and CXR are the initial laboratory and imaging studies. FNA (option B) is insufficient for lymphoma diagnosis — tissue architecture must be preserved to classify lymphoma subtype (Hodgkin vs non-Hodgkin, and further subclassification). Option A (antibiotics) delays diagnosis of a malignancy. Option D delays the inevitable biopsy unnecessarily given the clinical picture.

questions.stemAnchor: 26-year-old male + painless left testicular mass + beta-hCG elevated — testicular cancer urgent workup
questions.stem: A 26-year-old man presents concerned about a 'hard lump' he noticed in his left testicle 3 weeks ago. It is painless and has not changed in size. He denies trauma. His right testicle is normal. Examination reveals a 2 cm, firm, non-tender, non-transilluminating mass in the left testis. Scrotal ultrasound confirms a solid, hypoechoic mass. Beta-hCG is 450 mIU/mL (elevated). AFP is 18 ng/mL (normal). LDH is 220 U/L (slightly elevated). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Reassure the patient that a painless testicular mass is most likely a benign hydrocele and repeat ultrasound in 3 months
options.B: Refer urgently to urology for radical orchiectomy through an inguinal approach
options.C: Perform transscrotal needle biopsy to confirm malignancy before referral
options.D: Start empiric doxycycline for epididymo-orchitis and follow up in 2 weeks

questions.rationale: A solid, non-transilluminating testicular mass in a young male with elevated beta-hCG (marker for nonseminomatous germ cell tumor) is testicular cancer until proven otherwise. Radical orchiectomy via an INGUINAL approach is both diagnostic and therapeutic — transscrotal biopsy (option C) is contraindicated because it disrupts lymphatic drainage, potentially seeding inguinal nodes and changing staging/treatment. Hydrocele (option A) transilluminates — this does not, making the diagnosis unlikely. Epididymo-orchitis (option D) is painful; this is a painless mass. The elevated beta-hCG is a strong indicator of malignancy. Urgent urology referral is the correct action.

questions.stemAnchor: 52-year-old woman + nipple inversion + peau d'orange skin + breast erythema without fever — inflammatory breast cancer recognition
questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman presents with a 6-week history of redness, warmth, and swelling of her right breast. She has no fever. She notes the nipple has become inverted over the same time period, which is new. The skin of the right breast has a dimpled, orange-peel texture (peau d'orange). The breast is non-tender and there is no fluctuance. She was treated with antibiotics twice (cephalexin × 10 days, then clindamycin × 10 days) with no improvement. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Switch to IV vancomycin for MRSA mastitis and refer to infectious disease
options.B: Continue with a third antibiotic course (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) and recheck in 2 weeks
options.C: Refer urgently to breast surgery/oncology — this presentation is consistent with inflammatory breast cancer
options.D: Order breast ultrasound to identify an abscess for incision and drainage

questions.rationale: Inflammatory breast cancer (IBC) is a diagnosis frequently missed by being attributed to mastitis. Classic features: breast erythema + peau d'orange (dermal lymphatic invasion) + new nipple inversion + non-tender presentation + no improvement with antibiotics (2 failed antibiotic courses here). Mastitis is typically painful, tender, and resolves with antibiotics; it is most common postpartum. IBC does not require a palpable mass. Any antibiotic-non-responsive breast erythema with peau d'orange should be treated as IBC until proven otherwise with urgent oncology referral and skin punch biopsy. Options A and B delay malignancy diagnosis with further antibiotic courses.

questions.stemAnchor: 71-year-old + constant night back pain + known prostate cancer + bilateral leg numbness — malignant MSCC emergent steroids
questions.stem: A 71-year-old man with stage IV prostate cancer (on enzalutamide) presents with a 2-week worsening history of mid-back pain that is constant and worse at night. He notes bilateral leg tingling that began 4 days ago and has difficulty buttoning his shirt due to finger weakness. He has 4/5 bilateral lower extremity strength, hyperreflexia, and a positive Babinski bilaterally. Sensation is diminished in a band-like pattern across his mid-abdomen. Bladder and bowel function are currently intact. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.A: Increase enzalutamide dose and add a second hormonal agent for disease progression
options.B: Prescribe oxycodone for pain control and schedule outpatient spine MRI within 1 week
options.C: Administer dexamethasone 10–16 mg IV immediately and call 911 for emergent MRI and radiation oncology
options.D: Start naproxen and physical therapy referral for musculoskeletal back pain in a cancer patient

questions.rationale: Malignant spinal cord compression (MSCC) is an oncological emergency. This patient has: known metastatic prostate cancer + progressive back pain + bilateral hyperreflexia + Babinski sign + bilateral leg weakness + sensory level at mid-abdomen + upper limb involvement. Immediate dexamethasone 10–16 mg IV reduces cord edema and is the critical first action. Emergent MRI spine (full spine if possible) and radiation oncology evaluation must follow. Time to treatment determines neurological outcome — bladder/bowel function is currently intact, giving a brief window to act. Option B (opioid + outpatient MRI in 1 week) risks permanent paralysis. Options A and D are inappropriate for an emergency neurological presentation.

questions.stemAnchor: 48-year-old + hematuria no pain + heavy smoking history + occupational aniline dye — bladder cancer workup cystoscopy
questions.stem: A 48-year-old man presents after noticing dark-brown urine on 3 occasions over the past month. He denies pain, dysuria, or frequency. He is a 25-pack-year smoker and worked in a rubber manufacturing plant for 18 years. UA reveals 3+ blood with no protein, no nitrites, and no bacteria. Urine culture is negative. Creatinine is 1.0 mg/dL. Renal ultrasound is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Treat for occult UTI with ciprofloxacin and repeat UA in 3 weeks
options.B: Reassure that painless hematuria in men is usually benign and schedule follow-up in 6 months
options.C: Refer to urology for cystoscopy — painless gross hematuria in a high-risk patient requires bladder cancer exclusion
options.D: Order CT urogram to evaluate renal pelvis and ureters as the first diagnostic step

questions.rationale: Painless gross hematuria in a male is bladder cancer until proven otherwise. This patient has two major risk factors: heavy smoking (most important risk factor for bladder cancer) and occupational aromatic amine (aniline dye) exposure, a well-established bladder carcinogen. A normal renal ultrasound and negative culture do not exclude bladder cancer. Cystoscopy is the required diagnostic procedure — it allows direct visualization of the bladder mucosa and biopsy. CT urogram (option D) can complement cystoscopy but does not replace it for bladder lesions. Options A and B dangerously delay the cancer diagnosis. The NP must recognize this pattern and refer urgently to urology.

questions.stemAnchor: 63-year-old + 8% weight loss 3 months + normal initial cancer screen — CT chest/abdo/pelvis for unexplained weight loss
questions.stem: A 63-year-old man presents with unintentional weight loss of 18 pounds over 4 months (starting BMI 28, now 24). He denies fever, night sweats, change in bowel habits, rectal bleeding, hemoptysis, or dysphagia. He is a non-smoker. He underwent colonoscopy 2 years ago (normal), low-dose CT lung cancer screening 8 months ago (normal), and PSA is 1.4 ng/mL. CBC, CMP, TSH, and urinalysis are all normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Recommend dietary counseling and nutritional supplementation; repeat weight check in 3 months
options.B: Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast to evaluate for occult malignancy
options.C: Screen for depression and anxiety, as these are the most common causes of unintentional weight loss
options.D: Start mirtazapine 15 mg for appetite stimulation and follow up in 6 weeks

questions.rationale: Unexplained weight loss of ≥5% in 6 months or ≥10 pounds without identifiable cause after initial screening warrants CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to evaluate for occult malignancy. This patient has lost approximately 8% body weight over 4 months with a normal initial laboratory evaluation, recent normal colonoscopy and CT lung screen, and no other organ-specific symptoms. When initial screening is negative and the degree of weight loss is significant, CT cross-sectional imaging is the next appropriate step. Depression (option C) is the most common non-malignant cause of weight loss but should not be assumed without excluding malignancy given the degree of weight loss. Option D (appetite stimulant) addresses a symptom without seeking the cause.

questions.stemAnchor: Left shoulder pain + Horner syndrome + arm weakness in a smoker — Pancoast tumor superior sulcus recognition
questions.stem: A 59-year-old male smoker (45 pack-years) presents with a 3-month history of left shoulder pain radiating down the medial aspect of his left arm. He reports progressive weakness in his left hand grip. On exam: left ptosis, left miosis, left anhidrosis (confirmed on direct exam), and atrophy of the intrinsic hand muscles. CXR reveals an opacity at the left lung apex. Which of the following best describes this syndrome and the most appropriate next action?

options.A: Cervical disc herniation; order MRI cervical spine and refer to orthopedics
options.B: Brachial plexus injury from occupational strain; refer to physical therapy
options.C: Pancoast tumor (superior sulcus tumor); order CT chest with contrast and refer urgently to thoracic oncology
options.D: Cluster headache with associated Horner syndrome; start verapamil 240 mg daily

questions.rationale: Pancoast syndrome (superior sulcus tumor): apical lung mass + ipsilateral Horner syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis from compression of cervical sympathetic chain at the apex) + brachial plexopathy (medial arm pain, intrinsic hand muscle atrophy from C8/T1 involvement). The apical CXR opacity in a heavy smoker completes the picture. CT chest with contrast is the first imaging step; PET-CT and MRI brachial plexus will follow for staging. Cervical disc (option A) does not cause Horner syndrome. Cluster headache (option D) causes secondary Horner without lung apex mass or arm weakness. Recognition of this triad — Horner + arm pain/weakness + apical lung opacity in a smoker — is a high-yield NP certification examination topic.

questions.stemAnchor: 3-day-old neonate + bilious vomiting + abdominal distension — malrotation volvulus surgical emergency
questions.stem: A 3-day-old male neonate, born at term via uncomplicated vaginal delivery, is brought in by his mother because of two episodes of green-colored vomiting in the past 4 hours. He has been breastfeeding but now refuses to latch. Abdomen appears mildly distended. He has passed one meconium stool on day 1. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.A: Switch to hypoallergenic formula and follow up in 48 hours for feeding reassessment
options.B: Order an upper GI contrast series as an outpatient study within 24 hours to evaluate for malrotation
options.C: Call 911 for immediate transfer to a pediatric surgical center — bilious vomiting in a neonate is malrotation with midgut volvulus until proven otherwise
options.D: Reassure the mother that some neonatal vomiting is normal; evaluate for GERD and start ranitidine

questions.rationale: Bilious (green) vomiting in any neonate is a surgical emergency — malrotation with midgut volvulus until proven otherwise. Midgut volvulus presents typically in the first month of life and can progress to bowel infarction and death within hours if not surgically corrected. The NP must NOT delay for outpatient imaging or nutritional adjustment — immediate transfer to a pediatric surgical center is required. Option B (outpatient upper GI in 24 hours) would risk bowel necrosis. GERD (option D) is not bilious and is a diagnosis of exclusion. The rule is simple and absolute: bilious vomiting in a neonate = surgical emergency.

questions.stemAnchor: 6-week-old with fever 38.2 + no source — full septic workup admission rule <28 days
questions.stem: A mother brings her 6-week-old (42 days old) infant to your clinic with a rectal temperature of 38.2°C taken at home. The infant appears alert and is feeding normally. She has no cough, rhinorrhea, or diarrhea. Her fontanelle is flat. She was born at 38 weeks with no NICU stay. Which of the following best describes the appropriate management approach for this febrile infant?

options.A: Diagnose viral illness clinically, advise acetaminophen, and follow up if fever persists >72 hours
options.B: Order CBC, CMP, and UA; if low-risk criteria are met, discharge with 24-hour follow-up
options.C: Call 911 for immediate ED transfer — any infant under 28 days with fever ≥38°C requires a full septic workup and admission regardless of appearance
options.D: Prescribe amoxicillin empirically for otitis media and bring back in 3 days

questions.rationale: This infant is 42 days old — beyond the strict 28-day threshold for automatic full septic workup and admission (applies to <28 days). For infants 29–60 days with fever, risk stratification protocols (Philadelphia, Boston, Rochester criteria) can guide safe outpatient management if all low-risk criteria are met (well-appearing, no source, low WBC, clean UA, reliable caregiver with 24-hour follow-up). If this infant were <28 days, option C would be correct. At 42 days with a well-appearing infant, a step-down protocol is appropriate. Option A (no workup) is inappropriate — febrile infants under 3 months require at minimum CBC/UA/urine culture. Option D (empiric amoxicillin) without a source exam or workup is inappropriate.

questions.stemAnchor: 4-month-old + posterior rib fractures on XR + no mechanism given — non-accidental trauma mandatory reporting
questions.stem: A 4-month-old girl is brought in by her father for 'fussiness' for 3 days. CXR obtained to evaluate for respiratory infection incidentally reveals healing bilateral posterior rib fractures at multiple levels. Her father states she 'fell off the couch' 2 weeks ago. She is alert, has no external bruising, and currently appears comfortable. Developmental milestone: she cannot roll over. Which of the following is the most appropriate action?

options.A: Accept the explanation — infants can sustain rib fractures from falls off furniture
options.B: Order a full skeletal survey, retinal examination, and head CT; report suspected non-accidental trauma to Child Protective Services
options.C: Reassure the father that healing rib fractures are common in active infants and repeat CXR in 3 weeks
options.D: Refer to orthopedics for evaluation of possible osteogenesis imperfecta as the most likely cause of multiple rib fractures

questions.rationale: Posterior rib fractures in an infant are highly specific for non-accidental trauma (NAT/child abuse). The mechanism: squeezing the infant's chest during shaking compresses the posterior rib head against the transverse process. A 4-month-old cannot roll over — she cannot fall off a couch independently, and even if she did, a couch fall does not generate sufficient force for multiple posterior rib fractures. NAT workup: full skeletal survey, retinal exam (hemorrhages from shaking), and head MRI/CT (intracranial injury). Mandatory report to Child Protective Services is legally required — on suspicion, not confirmation. Osteogenesis imperfecta (option D) can cause fractures but would be associated with blue sclera, hearing loss, and dentinogenesis imperfecta — the forensic context here makes NAT the primary concern.

questions.stemAnchor: 2-day-old neonate + dusky color when feeding + improves with crying + SpO2 75% — ductus-dependent congenital heart disease
questions.stem: A 2-day-old male is brought in by his mother, who reports he turns 'blue around his lips' every time he feeds. She notes he calms and 'pinks up' when he cries. He was born at 39 weeks and passed newborn screen the day prior at home birth. SpO2 on pulse oximetry is 75% on room air. HR is 168. Lung exam is clear bilaterally. There is a soft 2/6 systolic murmur at the upper left sternal border. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.A: Prescribe hypoallergenic formula for milk protein allergy as the cause of feeding difficulty
options.B: Start supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula and schedule cardiology referral for next week
options.C: Call 911 immediately — SpO2 75% in a neonate with cyanosis that worsens with feeding but improves with crying suggests a ductus-dependent congenital heart lesion requiring prostaglandin E1
options.D: Reassure the mother that this is central cyanosis from normal neonatal transition; recheck at the 1-month well child visit

questions.rationale: Cyanosis that worsens with feeding but improves with crying is a classic sign of a ductus-dependent congenital heart lesion (e.g., transposition of the great arteries, critical pulmonary stenosis, hypoplastic left heart syndrome). SpO2 of 75% in a 2-day-old is critically low. As the ductus arteriosus closes in the first days of life, infants with ductus-dependent circulation become critically hypoxic. Emergency management: prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) to maintain ductal patency — administered only in an ICU setting. Supplemental oxygen alone (option B) is insufficient and may actually accelerate ductal closure in some lesions. The NP must call 911 immediately. This is a pediatric cardiac surgical emergency.

questions.stemAnchor: 12-month-old + any bruise + not yet walking — bruising in non-mobile infant NAT concern
questions.stem: A 12-month-old boy is brought in for a well child visit. He is not yet walking but pulls to stand. Mom reports he has been 'super active' and 'bumps into everything.' On exam, you note a 2 cm purple bruise on his inner left thigh and a 1.5 cm bruise on his left ear. The family history is unremarkable. The mother explains both are from 'everyday bumps.' Coagulation studies (PT, PTT, platelet count) are normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate response?

options.A: Document the bruises as consistent with normal activity in a mobile 12-month-old and complete the well child visit
options.B: Accept the explanation; bruising of a mobile child in soft tissue areas is expected
options.C: Report suspected non-accidental trauma to Child Protective Services; obtain skeletal survey and ophthalmology referral
options.D: Order repeat coagulation studies in 4 weeks to evaluate for bleeding disorder before escalating

questions.rationale: The 'TEN-4' rule: bruising on the Torso, Ears, or Neck in a child ≤4 years, OR ANY bruising in a child <4 months, is concerning for non-accidental trauma (NAT). A 12-month-old who does not yet walk independently cannot generate sufficient force through independent activity to bruise the inner thigh or ear. Ear bruising in any child who is not independently ambulatory is a high-specificity indicator of NAT. Normal coagulation studies make bleeding diathesis less likely but do not exclude it fully — a hematology referral and further workup should accompany the NAT workup. Mandatory CPS report is required. Option A incorrectly labels these as normal activity bruises in a non-walking child. Option D delays mandatory reporting.

questions.stemAnchor: Term neonate day 3 + no meconium passed + abdominal distension + rectal exam triggers explosive stool — Hirschsprung disease
questions.stem: A 3-day-old term male has not passed meconium since delivery. He has progressive abdominal distension and has been vomiting. On rectal exam, your finger reveals a narrow anal canal, and on withdrawal you trigger an explosive release of stool and gas. CXR shows dilated bowel loops without free air. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis and next step?

options.A: Normal neonatal constipation; recommend glycerin suppository and follow up in 48 hours
options.B: Meconium ileus from cystic fibrosis; order sweat chloride test and arrange outpatient GI referral
options.C: Hirschsprung disease; urgent pediatric surgical referral for rectal biopsy to confirm aganglionosis
options.D: Necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC); transfer to NICU immediately for IV antibiotics and NPO

questions.rationale: Hirschsprung disease (congenital aganglionosis) classic presentation: failure to pass meconium in the first 48 hours of life + abdominal distension + vomiting + explosive passage of stool on rectal examination (as the examiner's finger decompresses the aganglionic narrowed segment). The 'squirt sign' on rectal exam is classic. Urgent pediatric surgical consultation and rectal suction biopsy (absence of ganglion cells) confirm the diagnosis. NEC (option D) presents with bloody stools, pneumatosis intestinalis on XR, and systemic illness — free air if perforation occurs. Meconium ileus (option B) associated with CF does not show the explosive rectal exam decompression. Glycerin suppository (option A) would be insufficient for an aganglionic segment.

questions.stemAnchor: 5-month-old girl + intermittent inconsolable crying + vomiting + currant jelly stool — intussusception ileocolic
questions.stem: A 5-month-old girl is brought in by her father. She has been having intermittent 15-minute episodes of crying with legs pulled up to her chest, followed by periods of normalcy, for the past 8 hours. She vomited twice. Her father reports finding red, jelly-like material in her diaper this morning. She appears lethargic in your office between episodes. Abdomen is soft with mild RUQ fullness. Which of the following is the most appropriate action?

options.A: Diagnose colic; reassure and recommend simethicone drops
options.B: Diagnose gastroenteritis; recommend oral rehydration and follow up if not improved in 3 days
options.C: Call 911 for immediate transfer — currant jelly stool + episodic colicky pain + lethargy in a 5-month-old is intussusception until proven otherwise
options.D: Obtain abdominal X-ray in your clinic to assess for obstruction before calling for transfer

questions.rationale: Intussusception (typically ileocolic) classic triad: episodic colicky pain with intermittent well-appearing periods + vomiting + currant jelly stool (blood-tinged mucus from bowel wall ischemia — a LATE sign). Lethargy between episodes is a worrisome sign indicating hemodynamic compromise. Most common in 5-month to 3-year-old infants. Emergent transfer for ultrasound (gold standard diagnosis) and air or contrast enema reduction. Currant jelly stool indicates mucosal ischemia — delayed treatment risks perforation. Colic (option A) does not cause currant jelly stool. Gastroenteritis (option B) does not cause episodic pain or currant jelly stool. Delaying transfer for an in-office X-ray (option D) is inappropriate given the severity of the presentation.

questions.stemAnchor: 4-year-old + fever 5 days + strawberry tongue + bilateral conjunctivitis + diffuse rash + right cervical node 2 cm — Kawasaki disease IVIG decision
questions.stem: A 4-year-old girl has had fever to 39.8°C for 6 days that has not responded to antipyretics. On exam: bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue, cracked erythematous lips, diffuse polymorphous rash on her trunk, and a right-sided 2 cm cervical lymph node. Hands and feet show erythema and early edema. Labs: CRP 7.4 mg/dL, ESR 88 mm/hr, WBC 18,000, platelet count 580,000. Which of the following best represents the appropriate management?

options.A: Treat with amoxicillin for Group A Streptococcal scarlet fever and follow up in 48 hours
options.B: Reassure that the findings are consistent with viral exanthem; schedule return if fever persists beyond 10 days
options.C: Administer IVIG 2 g/kg + aspirin and arrange urgent pediatric cardiology evaluation for echocardiogram
options.D: Order monospot test and EBV serology for infectious mononucleosis as the most likely diagnosis

questions.rationale: Classic Kawasaki disease is present: fever ≥5 days + 4 of 5 CRASH criteria — Conjunctivitis (bilateral non-purulent) ✓, Rash ✓, Adenopathy (cervical >1.5 cm) ✓, Strawberry tongue/lip changes ✓, Hand/foot erythema ✓. This meets complete Kawasaki criteria. IVIG 2 g/kg (reduces coronary artery aneurysm risk from 25% untreated to <5%) + aspirin is the treatment. Echocardiogram at diagnosis is required to evaluate coronary arteries. Scarlet fever (option A) has sandpaper rash + pharyngitis + Group A Strep — no conjunctivitis, no extremity changes. EBV (option D) causes posterior cervical adenopathy + splenomegaly + heterophile-positive mono — different pattern. Viral exanthem (option B) does not persist ≥5 days with this multi-system pattern.

questions.stemAnchor: 3-year-old + sudden stridor + drooling + tripod position + high fever — epiglottitis do not examine throat
questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy is brought in by his parents with a 12-hour history of progressively worsening difficulty breathing and 'noisy breathing.' His mother reports he started drooling and refuses to swallow. Temp is 39.9°C. He is sitting in a tripod position (leaning forward, hands on knees). You hear audible inspiratory stridor. His voice is muffled. Which of the following represents the most appropriate immediate action?

options.A: Perform a throat exam with a tongue depressor to assess the epiglottis and pharynx
options.B: Order a stat lateral neck X-ray to look for the 'thumbprint sign' before any other action
options.C: Call 911 immediately — do NOT examine the throat or agitate the child; maintain calm until ENT/anesthesia arrives for airway management
options.D: Administer racemic epinephrine and dexamethasone for croup and observe for 2 hours

questions.rationale: This is epiglottitis until proven otherwise: high fever + stridor + drooling + muffled voice + tripod position in a young child. Critical rule: do NOT examine the throat with a tongue depressor — stimulating the inflamed epiglottis can trigger complete laryngospasm and immediate airway obstruction. The child must be kept calm, upright, with the parent present. ENT and anesthesia must manage the airway in a controlled setting (OR). Call 911 immediately. Croup (option D) presents with barky cough, low-grade fever, and responds to racemic epinephrine/dexamethasone — drooling and tripod positioning are not croup features. Lateral neck X-ray showing 'thumbprint sign' confirms epiglottitis but should not delay airway management call.

questions.stemAnchor: 2-year-old + witnessed choking + sudden onset wheeze + unilateral air trapping — foreign body aspiration
questions.stem: A 2-year-old girl is brought in by her mother, who witnessed her choking on a peanut 30 minutes ago. After a coughing spell, the child appeared to recover and is now breathing but has developed a new wheeze. SpO2 is 95%. Physical exam reveals diminished breath sounds on the left with a persistent monophonic wheeze. CXR (expiratory film) shows left lung hyperinflation compared to the right. Which of the following is the most appropriate action?

options.A: Prescribe albuterol MDI and follow up in 24 hours for 'first episode of asthma'
options.B: Perform abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver) since the foreign body has not been expelled
options.C: Call 911 for urgent transfer for bronchoscopic evaluation and removal of the aspirated foreign body
options.D: Order CT chest to localize the foreign body before referring to surgery in 48 hours

questions.rationale: Foreign body aspiration (FBA): witnessed choking + sudden onset wheeze + unilateral decreased breath sounds + unilateral air trapping (from ball-valve obstruction — the foreign body allows air in but not out, causing air trapping on expiratory film). Bronchoscopy is the definitive diagnostic and therapeutic procedure — it must be done urgently (not emergently unless complete obstruction). Do NOT perform Heimlich maneuver (option B) on a child who is still breathing and coughing — Heimlich is for complete obstruction (child cannot breathe or cough). Albuterol (option A) would worsen air trapping. Delayed CT and surgery referral in 48 hours (option D) risks distal migration, erosion, or complete obstruction. The child needs urgent airway management.

questions.stemAnchor: 8-year-old + migratory arthritis + new murmur + recent strep pharyngitis — acute rheumatic fever Jones criteria
questions.stem: A 8-year-old girl presents with swollen, painful joints that have migrated: first the right knee, then the left ankle, now the right wrist over a 10-day period. She had a sore throat treated with a 5-day course of amoxicillin 3 weeks ago (not confirmed strep — 'given for precaution'). On exam she has a new 3/6 holosystolic murmur at the apex. Temp 38.1°C. ESR 68 mm/hr, CRP 3.8 mg/dL. ASO titer is 420 IU/mL (elevated). ECG shows PR interval of 240 ms. Which of the following is the most appropriate action?

options.A: Treat with ibuprofen and advise the parents this is reactive arthritis from a viral illness
options.B: Refer for echocardiogram and initiate penicillin G + aspirin; diagnose acute rheumatic fever (ARF)
options.C: Order ANA and anti-CCP antibodies to evaluate for juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA)
options.D: Diagnose viral myocarditis and admit to the PICU for cardiac monitoring
