questions.rationale: This patient meets Jones criteria for acute rheumatic fever (ARF): Major criteria — migratory polyarthritis ✓ + carditis (new murmur + prolonged PR) ✓ = 2 major criteria; plus evidence of prior GAS infection (elevated ASO titer). ARF Jones criteria: 2 major OR 1 major + 2 minor (fever, elevated ESR/CRP, prolonged PR, elevated WBC) + evidence of streptococcal infection. Treatment: penicillin G to eradicate residual GAS + aspirin for arthritis/carditis. Echocardiogram to assess mitral valve involvement (subclinical carditis). Long-term secondary prophylaxis with monthly benzathine penicillin is required. JIA (option C) does not explain the carditis or elevated ASO. Viral myocarditis (option D) does not cause migratory arthritis or elevated ASO.

questions.stemAnchor: 10-year-old + baseball to head + lost consciousness 30 seconds + vomited once + GCS 15 — PECARN CT decision rule
questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy was hit in the right temporal region by a baseball 2 hours ago. He lost consciousness for approximately 30 seconds, then became alert. He vomited once. He now has a headache rated 4/10. GCS is 15. There is a soft tissue swelling over the right temporal region. He is alert, oriented, and following commands. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Apply the PECARN rule: this patient has LOC AND vomiting — he requires CT head to exclude intracranial injury
options.B: He is GCS 15 and currently well-appearing; PECARN classifies him as low risk; observe 4–6 hours without CT
options.C: Discharge home with standard concussion precautions since he is asymptomatic now
options.D: Administer acetaminophen and schedule repeat neurological exam tomorrow

questions.rationale: PECARN pediatric head CT decision rule for children ≥2 years: CT is recommended if ANY of these high-risk factors are present: GCS <15, signs of skull fracture (palpable, non-frontal scalp hematoma, battle sign, hemotympanum), altered mental status, or severe mechanism. Observation is preferred if: isolated LOC <5 seconds OR isolated vomiting OR non-severe mechanism — but NOT if multiple factors are present. This patient has BOTH LOC (30 seconds — longer than trivial 5 seconds) AND vomiting — two intermediate-risk factors together warrant CT or at minimum prolonged observation. The temporal swelling (temporal bone = thin) adds concern for temporal epidural hematoma risk. In a child with LOC >5 seconds + vomiting + temporal hematoma, CT head is appropriate. Option B would be correct if these were isolated findings; combined, CT is warranted.

questions.stemAnchor: 16-year-old + sore throat + anterior cervical LAD + bilateral tonsillar exudate + rash after amoxicillin — EBV mono amoxicillin rash
questions.stem: A 16-year-old girl presents with a 5-day history of severe sore throat, fatigue, and difficulty swallowing. Exam reveals: temperature 38.6°C, bilateral anterior and posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, significantly enlarged tonsils with exudate bilaterally, splenomegaly on palpation. Rapid strep test is negative. Her primary care provider prescribed amoxicillin 500 mg TID 3 days ago. She now presents with a diffuse maculopapular rash covering her trunk, arms, and legs. Which of the following best explains the rash?

options.A: True penicillin allergy — amoxicillin has caused an IgE-mediated allergic reaction; label her as penicillin-allergic
options.B: Amoxicillin-induced rash in EBV infectious mononucleosis — this is NOT a true penicillin allergy; do not label her penicillin-allergic
options.C: Viral exanthem from EBV itself — the rash is unrelated to the amoxicillin
options.D: Scarlet fever from Group A Strep despite negative rapid strep — start cephalexin as alternative antibiotic

questions.rationale: The amoxicillin rash in EBV infectious mononucleosis is a classic teaching point: approximately 80–100% of patients with EBV mono who receive amoxicillin or ampicillin develop a diffuse maculopapular rash. This is NOT a true penicillin allergy — it is a drug-virus interaction caused by immune activation of EBV-infected B cells. Falsely labeling this patient as penicillin-allergic would deprive her of future use of first-line antibiotics for many infections. The rash disappears as the mono resolves. This question also tests recognition that the negative rapid strep + bilateral posterior adenopathy + splenomegaly + this rash pattern = EBV mono. The Phase 1 question np-p1-065 (fever topic) specifically addressed this distinction — this question reinforces the same concept in a different context (the actual rash presentation vs the diagnostic workup).

questions.stemAnchor: 8-year-old + fever 4 days + bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis + rash + 3 of 5 CRASH criteria — incomplete Kawasaki
questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy has had fever for 5 days (max 39.5°C). On exam: bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis and a polymorphous rash on his trunk are present. His lips are not cracked and his tongue is normal. No lymphadenopathy is noted. Hands show no erythema. CBC: WBC 18,000, platelets 620,000. CRP 9.2 mg/dL. ESR 96 mm/hr. Echocardiogram is requested. Which of the following diagnosis most accurately describes this presentation?

options.A: Complete Kawasaki disease; meets all 5 criteria
options.B: Incomplete Kawasaki disease; fever ≥5 days + 2 classic criteria + elevated acute phase reactants warrant echo and possible IVIG
options.C: Viral exanthem with reactive thrombocytosis; observe and follow up in 3 days
options.D: Systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis (sJIA) with fever and rash; refer to pediatric rheumatology

questions.rationale: Incomplete (atypical) Kawasaki disease: fever ≥5 days + 2–3 classic criteria (conjunctivitis + rash = 2) without meeting the 4-of-5 full Kawasaki criteria. Per AHA guidelines, incomplete Kawasaki should be considered when fever ≥5 days is present with 2 or more classic features + elevated acute phase reactants (CRP ≥3 mg/dL, ESR ≥40 mm/hr). Echocardiography is required — evidence of coronary artery abnormalities (Z score ≥2.5) confirms Kawasaki regardless of clinical criteria count. IVIG should be administered if echo shows coronary changes or if clinical + lab picture strongly supports incomplete KD. Viral exanthem (option C) does not explain 5-day fever with CRP 9.2 + ESR 96. sJIA (option D) presents with quotidian fever, salmon-colored rash, and arthritis — different pattern.

questions.stemAnchor: 24-year-old + PHQ-9 item 9 response + plan with firearm access + LGBTQ+ identity — C-SSRS stratification involuntary hold
questions.stem: A 24-year-old transgender male presents for follow-up of MDD. PHQ-9 score is 21 (severe). On item 9, he scores 3 ('thoughts that you would be better off dead or of hurting yourself in some way — nearly every day'). During the C-SSRS, he endorses active suicidal ideation with a specific plan (intending to use his roommate's firearm) and states he has been thinking about this daily for 2 weeks. He has no prior suicide attempts. He declines voluntary hospitalization. Which of the following is the most appropriate action?

options.A: Document the conversation, provide the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline number, and increase his antidepressant dose
options.B: Create a safety plan with him and schedule a follow-up appointment in 3 days
options.C: Initiate an involuntary psychiatric hold (5150/Baker Act) — active ideation with plan and means access constitutes imminent danger to self
options.D: Recommend he ask his roommate to store the firearm elsewhere and discharge with a safety plan

questions.rationale: This patient has C-SSRS active suicidal ideation WITH a specific plan (firearm) AND access to means — the highest-risk combination. Key criteria met for involuntary hold: (1) active plan with identified means, (2) access to firearm (lethal means — suicide completion rate by firearm is >85%), (3) daily ideation for 2 weeks. He declines voluntary hospitalization. Involuntary hold criteria in most states: danger to self + mental disorder + unable or unwilling to accept voluntary treatment. A safety plan and follow-up in 3 days (option B) is appropriate for LOW risk — not for active plan + lethal means access. Means restriction counseling (option D) is incomplete — he must be safe first. Increasing the antidepressant (option A) does not address the immediate risk.

questions.stemAnchor: 67-year-old widower + recent retirement + passive ideation only + no plan + strong religious objection — low-risk safety plan discharge
questions.stem: A 67-year-old widowed man comes in for a follow-up visit after his wife died 8 weeks ago. PHQ-9 score is 18 (moderately severe). He endorses: 'I've had thoughts that I'd be okay with dying, like if God took me in my sleep.' He denies active thoughts of suicide, has no plan, and has never made an attempt. He is Catholic and states suicide would be a mortal sin — he has no intention of harming himself. He has two adult children who visit weekly. He is engaged in our conversation and tearful but interactive. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Hospitalize involuntarily — any suicidal ideation in an elderly widowed male requires inpatient care
options.B: Discharge with a safety plan, initiation of antidepressant therapy, and follow-up within 1–2 weeks
options.C: Discharge without any intervention since his religious beliefs preclude suicide
options.D: Refer to psychiatry for outpatient evaluation in 2 months since this is grief, not depression

questions.rationale: This patient has passive suicidal ideation (wishes to die without active intent, plan, or means) with multiple strong protective factors: religious objection to suicide (one of the strongest protective factors), engaged adult family support, interactive and cooperative with care, no prior attempts. He does NOT meet involuntary hold criteria — the hold requires danger to self, which requires active intent/plan or grave disability. Appropriate management: safety plan (document protective factors, provide crisis line, agree on follow-up plan), initiate antidepressant (PHQ-9 ≥15 with functional impairment), and close follow-up within 1–2 weeks. Option A (involuntary hold) is disproportionate for passive ideation with strong protective factors. Option D delays needed intervention.

questions.stemAnchor: 38-year-old male on sertraline + just lost job + recent divorce + asks about medication lethal dose — high-risk means assessment
questions.stem: A 38-year-old man on sertraline 100 mg (prescribed by you for MDD) calls in requesting a 90-day prescription refill. During the call he mentions he recently lost his job and his divorce was finalized last week. He then asks, 'How many of these pills would it take to be harmful if someone took too many at once?' Which of the following is the most appropriate response?

options.A: Answer his question clinically: 'SSRIs have a high therapeutic index and are rarely fatal even in overdose' and send the 90-day refill
options.B: Ignore the question and send a 30-day refill with follow-up in 4 weeks
options.C: Screen immediately with C-SSRS; do NOT refill 90 days until risk assessment is completed; limit supply and arrange urgent face-to-face evaluation today or tomorrow
options.D: Discontinue sertraline immediately as it may increase suicidal ideation and refer to crisis services

questions.rationale: This call contains multiple high-risk signals: recent major life stressors (job loss + divorce finalization) + a direct question about medication lethal dose = a screened sentinel event requiring immediate suicide risk assessment. Providing a 90-day supply of any psychiatric medication to a patient who may be researching lethality is contraindicated. The NP should: (1) conduct an immediate phone-based C-SSRS assessment, (2) limit supply to a small quantity pending in-person evaluation, (3) arrange urgent face-to-face evaluation within 24 hours, (4) if the patient endorses active ideation with plan — escalate to crisis line or emergency services. Option A provides lethality information to a potentially at-risk patient. Option B ignores the red flag entirely. Option D abruptly discontinuing sertraline can worsen depression and increase risk.

questions.stemAnchor: Patient expresses intent to harm identified former coworker by name + access to firearm — Tarasoff duty to warn
questions.stem: A 45-year-old male patient with major depressive disorder presents for a follow-up visit. He becomes increasingly agitated when discussing a former coworker he holds responsible for his recent termination. He states: 'I know where David (full name provided) lives, and I have my gun in my car right now. I've been driving by his house every night. One of these nights I'm going to make him pay.' Which of the following actions is legally and ethically required of the NP?

options.A: Document the statement in the medical record and notify the patient's family only
options.B: Maintain confidentiality — disclosing this information would violate HIPAA
options.C: Notify the identified potential victim AND law enforcement; evaluate the patient for involuntary hold
options.D: Refer the patient to psychiatry for outpatient evaluation within 1 week and increase his antidepressant

questions.rationale: This is a Tarasoff duty-to-warn scenario. The Tarasoff ruling (California 1974) establishes the legal duty for mental health providers: if a patient makes a specific, credible threat to an identifiable third party, the provider must (1) notify the potential victim, and (2) notify law enforcement. Elements present: specific threat + identified victim by name + stated means (firearm in car) + stalking behavior (driving by his house nightly) + stated intent. This obligation overrides HIPAA confidentiality in most jurisdictions. The patient must also be evaluated for involuntary hold (danger to others). Documenting and waiting (option A) is inadequate. Family notification (option A) does not satisfy the legal Tarasoff duty. Option D dangerously delays action on an imminent threat.

questions.stemAnchor: 16-year-old LGBTQ+ teen + recent school bullying + PHQ-9 severe + previous attempt 1 year ago — high-risk adolescent assessment
questions.stem: A 16-year-old gender non-conforming adolescent is brought in by a parent after their school counselor expressed concern. PHQ-9 score is 22 (severe). They disclose severe bullying at school for the past 3 months about their gender identity. They attempted an overdose with acetaminophen one year ago and were hospitalized for 3 days. They deny current active ideation but state 'I've been thinking a lot about how there's no point to anything.' C-SSRS reveals passive ideation without current plan or intent. Which of the following best characterizes the risk level and appropriate management?

options.A: Low risk — no active ideation; reassure and schedule routine follow-up in 6 weeks
options.B: Moderate-high risk — prior attempt + LGBTQ+ identity + ongoing bullying + severe PHQ-9 warrants urgent psychiatric evaluation within 24–48 hours
options.C: Involuntary hold is required because any prior suicide attempter with passive ideation must be hospitalized
options.D: Refer to school counseling and address bullying as the primary intervention; schedule follow-up in 1 month

questions.rationale: This adolescent's risk stratification: prior attempt (strongest single predictor of future attempt) + LGBTQ+ identity without affirming support (3× higher suicide risk) + ongoing psychosocial stressor (bullying) + severe PHQ-9 (22) + passive ideation. While passive ideation without current plan does not mandate involuntary hold, the cumulative risk factors place this patient in the moderate-high risk category requiring urgent psychiatric evaluation. Same-day or next-day psychiatric consultation is appropriate. Option A underestimates the risk — a prior attempter with ongoing stressors and severe depression should never be classified as low risk. Option C (automatic hold for passive ideation) is incorrect — the hold threshold requires active danger or grave disability, not passive ideation alone. Option D inadequately addresses the psychiatric severity.

questions.stemAnchor: Patient taking TCA + expresses SI + asks for 3-month supply — lethal means restriction prescription limit
questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with chronic MDD is being managed on nortriptyline 75 mg (a tricyclic antidepressant) with partial response. She discloses passive suicidal ideation today — wishes she wasn't alive, no plan, no intent, no access to firearms. Her PHQ-9 is 14. She requests a 90-day refill for her nortriptyline prescription. Which of the following is the most appropriate prescribing decision?

options.A: Provide a 90-day supply to reduce pharmacy trips and improve medication access
options.B: Switch to an SSRI immediately since TCAs should never be prescribed in patients with any suicidal ideation
options.C: Provide a 2–4 week supply only; arrange close follow-up within 2 weeks; consider guardian-supervised dispensing
options.D: Prescribe as requested; the passive ideation is low risk and does not affect prescribing decisions

questions.rationale: Tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs) are among the most lethal medications in overdose — a 10-day supply can be a lethal dose (cardiotoxicity from sodium channel blockade). Any patient with suicidal ideation on a TCA should receive restricted quantities. The principle of lethal means restriction applied to prescribing: limit supply to 2–4 weeks maximum in the presence of any suicidal ideation; consider having a trusted family member hold the medication (guardian-supervised dispensing). A 90-day supply (option A) places a lethal quantity within reach. Option B (switch to SSRI) may ultimately be appropriate but is a separate clinical decision — the immediate action is supply restriction. Option D ignores an evidence-based prescribing safety principle.

questions.stemAnchor: Native American 58-year-old + alcohol use disorder + recent incarceration + isolation + passive SI — population-specific high-risk identification
questions.stem: A 58-year-old Native American man presents for management of alcohol use disorder and depression following release from 6-month incarceration 3 weeks ago. He is divorced, lives alone on a rural reservation, and reports he has no close contacts in the area. He denies active suicidal ideation but describes feeling 'invisible' and says 'nobody would miss me.' PHQ-9 score is 16. He is not on any medications. Which of the following represents the most accurate risk characterization and appropriate response?

options.A: Low risk — no active ideation and a PHQ-9 of 16 alone does not require escalation
options.B: Moderate-high risk — multiple high-risk markers for suicide completion present; initiate treatment, safety plan, and close follow-up within 1–2 weeks with outreach support
options.C: Involuntary hold is required based on the passive 'nobody would miss me' statement alone
options.D: Address alcohol use disorder only and schedule mental health follow-up in 3 months

questions.rationale: This patient has multiple population-specific high-risk factors for suicide completion: (1) Native American male (highest suicide rate of any US demographic group), (2) alcohol use disorder (3× increased suicide risk), (3) recent incarceration (transition period = very high risk), (4) social isolation (rural, no support network), (5) divorce, (6) PHQ-9 16 (moderately severe), (7) passive ideation ('nobody would miss me' is a warning sign). Passive ideation alone does not mandate involuntary hold, but this patient's cumulative profile requires urgent action: initiate antidepressant + alcohol use disorder treatment, comprehensive safety plan with crisis resources, and close follow-up within 1–2 weeks with built-in outreach (phone check-in). Option A grossly underestimates risk in a high-risk demographic. Option C incorrectly applies involuntary hold criteria. Option D defers mental health care inappropriately.

questions.stemAnchor: 48-year-old + new psychosis + low sodium + recent steroid initiation + confusion — steroid-induced psychosis organic workup
questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with newly diagnosed temporal arteritis was started on prednisone 60 mg daily 5 days ago. Her husband brings her in because she has been talking to 'people who aren't there' since yesterday, is agitated, and had her first episode of disorganized behavior this morning. She had no prior psychiatric history. BMP: Na 128 mEq/L, K 3.0, Cr 0.9. TSH is normal. Glucose is 198 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?

options.A: Initiate haloperidol 5 mg IM immediately for acute psychotic agitation
options.B: Diagnose new-onset schizophrenia and refer urgently to psychiatry for antipsychotic initiation
options.C: Reduce prednisone dose and correct hyponatremia; these are the most likely organic causes of her acute psychosis
options.D: Order brain MRI with contrast to evaluate for temporal arteritis CNS involvement

questions.rationale: This is steroid-induced psychosis + hyponatremia — both organic causes. Prednisone 60 mg/day is a classic trigger for steroid psychosis (usually dose-dependent, onset typically days 5–10). Additionally, hyponatremia (Na 128) itself can cause confusion and psychosis. The correct approach is to treat the organic causes first: taper prednisone to the minimum effective dose for temporal arteritis (often 30–40 mg after initial dose) and correct hyponatremia. Diagnosing schizophrenia (option B) in a 48-year-old with no prior history who developed psychosis 5 days after starting high-dose steroids is premature and incorrect. Haloperidol (option A) may be needed if dangerous agitation persists after organic cause correction, but organic cause treatment is the priority. Option D (MRI for CNS vasculitis) is less urgent — organic drug/metabolic causes should be addressed first.

questions.stemAnchor: 22-year-old + 3 weeks progressive hallucinations + flat affect + social withdrawal + HIV-negative + normal labs — first-episode psychosis workup
questions.stem: A 22-year-old man is brought by his parents. Over 3 weeks he has been hearing voices that comment on his actions, believes the government has implanted a device in his head, and has become reclusive. He was an honors student until 6 months ago. Urine drug screen is negative. CBC, CMP, TSH, B12, RPR, and HIV are all negative. Brain MRI shows no abnormality. He has no family history of schizophrenia. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Initiate risperidone 2 mg daily in your clinic and schedule follow-up in 6 weeks
options.B: Refer urgently to a first-episode psychosis (FEP) program or psychiatric evaluation for antipsychotic initiation under specialist supervision
options.C: Reassure the family that this is likely a late adolescent adjustment disorder and will resolve without medication
options.D: Order EEG to evaluate for complex partial seizures as a cause of his hallucinations

questions.rationale: This is a first-episode psychosis (FEP) presentation: gradual-onset positive symptoms (auditory hallucinations, paranoid delusions) + negative symptoms (flat affect, social withdrawal) + prodromal academic decline in a young adult with normal medical workup. First-episode psychosis evaluation and antipsychotic initiation should occur under psychiatric specialist supervision in a specialized FEP program — early coordinated care (NAVIGATE, RAISE studies) improves long-term outcomes. Initiating antipsychotics in primary care without psychiatric specialist input is outside NP scope for first-episode psychosis. Option A (risperidone in primary care) is inappropriate without specialist co-management. Option C (reassurance) dismisses a serious neuropsychiatric illness. Option D (EEG) may be part of a broader workup if seizure is suspected — but normal MRI and no motor symptoms make this lower priority.

questions.stemAnchor: Delirium vs dementia — elderly patient + acute fluctuating confusion vs slowly progressive — clinical distinction
questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with known Alzheimer's dementia (MMSE 18/30 at baseline) is brought in by her daughter. Over the past 2 days she has been 'not herself' — more confused than usual, talking to people who aren't there, and unable to recognize her daughter at times. She does not recognize her daughter today (new from baseline). Temperature is 38.3°C. Urinalysis reveals 3+ nitrites, 3+ leukocyte esterase. Which of the following is the most accurate clinical interpretation?

options.A: This represents Alzheimer's disease progression — no intervention beyond reassurance is needed
options.B: This is delirium superimposed on dementia, likely secondary to a urinary tract infection — treat the underlying cause
options.C: New-onset psychosis in dementia; initiate quetiapine for behavioral management
options.D: Sundowning behavior — provide nighttime melatonin and bright light therapy

questions.rationale: Delirium superimposed on dementia (DSD): an ACUTE change above a known dementia baseline — fluctuating consciousness, new hallucinations, worsening cognition that is ACUTE (2 days), with an identifiable medical cause (UTI = urinalysis strongly positive). Key distinguishing features: delirium = ACUTE fluctuating consciousness + identifiable precipitant; dementia = slowly progressive with stable fluctuation. This patient's sudden acute worsening beyond her baseline Alzheimer's level, triggered by fever and UTI, is classic DSD. Treatment is the underlying cause (UTI antibiotics) — the confusion should resolve as the infection clears. Option A (Alzheimer's progression) is incorrect for a 2-day acute change. Option C (quetiapine) for behavioral management in dementia is sometimes used but is not the first action when a treatable medical cause is identified. Sundowning (D) is a chronic nocturnal pattern — not acute 2-day worsening.

questions.stemAnchor: Agitated patient + threatening + refusal to leave + de-escalation failing — acute agitation management safety protocol
questions.stem: A 34-year-old man with a history of bipolar disorder presents demanding a specific controlled substance prescription. When you decline, he becomes verbally aggressive, stands up, and begins moving toward you. He is loud, unpredictable, and has pushed a chair. Other patients in the waiting room are visible through the window. De-escalation attempts (low voice, offering him a seat, naming his emotions) have been unsuccessful. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Attempt to physically restrain the patient to prevent injury to yourself and others
options.B: Increase the verbal de-escalation intensity and engage him one-on-one in a private room
options.C: Call 911 immediately; do not attempt physical restraint without security/law enforcement
options.D: Administer intramuscular haloperidol to manage acute agitation before law enforcement arrives

questions.rationale: Primary care clinic safety protocol for acute agitation that does not respond to de-escalation: call 911 immediately. Do NOT attempt physical restraint without trained security personnel — physical confrontation by clinical staff without restraint training increases injury risk for both the patient and the provider. Moving the patient to a private room (option B) isolates both parties and increases danger. Physical restraint by the NP alone (option A) is dangerous. IM haloperidol (option D) may be used for pharmacological calming in emergency settings with appropriate monitoring — but in a primary care clinic, this is outside safe practice without emergency medication protocols and monitoring capability. The correct response is call 911, maintain distance, exit with other patients to a safe area if possible.

questions.stemAnchor: 28-year-old + excited delirium + agitation + hyperthermia + superhuman strength + police physical restraint — risk of sudden death
questions.stem: A 28-year-old man is brought in by police who found him agitated, incoherent, and 'unbelievably strong' in a parking lot. He is profusely diaphoretic, temperature 40.2°C. He is tachycardic at 142 bpm. He is restrained and continues to struggle violently against physical restraints. He has no ID. Urine drug screen is positive for cocaine. Which of the following represents the most critical clinical concern and immediate action?

options.A: Administer IV saline for suspected heat exhaustion from physical exertion
options.B: Apply chemical sedation (benzodiazepine IM) and monitor; this is excited delirium syndrome with risk of sudden cardiac death during prone restraint
options.C: Apply additional physical restraints in prone position to protect staff from injury
options.D: Order CT head to evaluate for intracranial hemorrhage before any medication

questions.rationale: Excited delirium syndrome (ExDS): extreme agitation + hyperthermia + diaphoresis + tachycardia + superhuman strength + cocaine use. ExDS is associated with high risk of sudden cardiac death, particularly during or immediately following prone restraint (positional asphyxia + catecholamine surge). Critical management: (1) Chemical sedation with benzodiazepine (lorazepam or midazolam IM) is preferred to prolonged physical restraint — reduces the catecholamine storm and risk of cardiac arrest; (2) Avoid prone restraint once sedation is administered; (3) Monitor cardiac rhythm and temperature continuously; (4) IV saline for hyperthermia. Prone position (option C) is contraindicated in ExDS — it increases mortality. CT head (option D) is not the immediate priority — securing the airway and cardiovascular stability come first.

questions.stemAnchor: 35-year-old postpartum + 2 weeks postdelivery + hearing voices telling her to harm baby + refusing hospital — postpartum psychosis emergency
questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman, G2P2, is brought to your clinic by her husband 2 weeks after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery. Her husband reports she has not slept more than 1–2 hours per night for the past 5 days, has been talking 'nonsense,' and this morning told him 'the baby doesn't need me anymore.' She appears agitated and disheveled. She tells you directly that she hears a voice telling her the baby would be 'better off without her.' She refuses hospital admission. Which of the following is the most appropriate action?

options.A: Diagnose postpartum depression and prescribe sertraline with 2-week follow-up
options.B: Initiate an involuntary psychiatric hold — postpartum psychosis with infanticidal ideation and danger to self is a psychiatric emergency
options.C: Discharge home with her husband as the 'supportive caregiver' and arrange outpatient psychiatry within 1 week
options.D: Advise the husband to remove the baby from the home until the mother's mood improves

questions.rationale: Postpartum psychosis (PPP): onset within 2–4 weeks postpartum + sleep deprivation + disorganized behavior + auditory hallucinations + command hallucinations related to the infant + danger to self (and potential infant) — this is a psychiatric emergency distinct from postpartum depression. PPP is associated with 4–5% infanticide risk and significant suicide risk. Involuntary psychiatric hospitalization is required if the patient refuses voluntary admission, given the command hallucinations regarding the infant. Option A (sertraline) treats postpartum depression, not psychosis. Option C (discharge home with husband) places both mother and infant at immediate risk. Option D (remove baby) is an insufficient safety measure without inpatient psychiatric stabilization.

questions.stemAnchor: 55-year-old + new onset auditory hallucinations + RPR reactive 1:64 + history of unprotected sex — neurosyphilis psychiatric presentation
questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with no prior psychiatric history presents with new-onset auditory hallucinations and paranoid ideation over the past 3 months. He has also reported progressively worsening memory and personality change. He has a history of multiple sexual partners and does not consistently use condoms. His medical workup shows: RPR 1:64 (reactive), FTA-ABS positive. CSF analysis: VDRL positive, WBC 22 (lymphocytic). HIV is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment?

options.A: Start antipsychotic medication and refer to psychiatry for long-term management
options.B: Treat with benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million units IM weekly for 3 weeks (late latent syphilis protocol)
options.C: Initiate IV aqueous penicillin G 18–24 million units per day for 10–14 days — neurosyphilis protocol
options.D: Treat with doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 28 days — alternative to penicillin for neurosyphilis

questions.rationale: This is neurosyphilis presenting as psychiatric illness — auditory hallucinations, paranoia, and cognitive decline with CSF VDRL positive (specific for CNS infection). Psychiatric manifestations of neurosyphilis (meningovascular, parenchymatous, or tabes dorsalis) are organic and require antibiotics, not antipsychotics primarily. Treatment: IV aqueous penicillin G 18–24 million units/day for 10–14 days — NOT IM benzathine penicillin (which does not achieve adequate CSF levels). Option B (benzathine penicillin IM) is for late latent syphilis WITHOUT CNS involvement — benzathine penicillin does not penetrate the CSF adequately. Option D (doxycycline) has limited evidence for neurosyphilis and is not the preferred regimen. Option A (antipsychotics alone) misses the treatable infectious cause — the psychiatric symptoms can partially reverse with adequate antibiotics.
